message.content:
Add a "1Password access" doc to GitHub repo https://github.com/u2giants/authentik. `gh` is authed as u2giants. Use the Bash tool (Git Bash/POSIX). Work in C:/repos/temp/1p/authentik. The canonical doc body is at C:/repos/temp/1p/CANONICAL-1password.md — READ IT and use it verbatim. NOTE: this is likely a FORK of a large external project (authentik), so be least-intrusive.

STEPS:
1. Remove C:/repos/temp/1p/authentik if it exists, then `gh repo clone u2giants/authentik C:/repos/temp/1p/authentik -- --depth 1`. If clone fails or repo is empty, STOP and report.
2. Default branch: `gh repo view u2giants/authentik --json defaultBranchRef --jq .defaultBranchRef.name`. Also `gh repo view u2giants/authentik --json isFork,parent` to confirm fork status.
3. Read root `*.md` and any `docs/` index. Determine:
   a. MAP: is there a documentation map/index our team maintains (a section/file linking the repo's docs)? On a big upstream fork there usually is NOT one of "ours".
   b. PLACEMENT: because this is a fork of a large external project, prefer a STANDALONE `docs/1password.md` (create `docs/` only if the repo doesn't already use a different docs location; if it has a docs site dir like `website/` keep it simple and put the file at repo-root `docs/1password.md` or root `1PASSWORD.md` — pick the least surprising). Keep the `# Using 1Password in this repo` H1. Add ONE concise pointer line near the TOP of README.md (a short blockquote: "> Internal: see [docs/1password.md](docs/1password.md) for 1Password access (MCP server + op CLI)."). Do NOT restructure or rewrite the upstream README.
4. Write the doc and add the single pointer line.
5. If there is genuinely no "our" documentation map, FLAG "NO MAP" in your report (expected for a big upstream fork).
6. DELIVERY:
   a. Create branch `docs/1password-access` from the default branch; commit ONLY the doc + the one-line README pointer. Message: `docs: add 1Password MCP + op CLI usage guide` with trailer `Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 <noreply@anthropic.com>`. Push; open a PR vs the default branch (`gh pr create`, body ends with `🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)`).
   b. IF push/PR is blocked by a repository ruleset forbidding new branches (rejection mentioning ruleset / "cannot create ref" / GH013 / protected) → FALL BACK: checkout the default branch, commit the same changes there, push the default branch. Report you used a DIRECT COMMIT and why.
   c. If BOTH fail, report the exact error.
7. Modify nothing else. No real secret values.

RETURN terse: repo; fork? (yes/no); placement (path + pointer location); map status ("NO MAP — flagged" if none); delivery (PR URL, or "direct commit to <branch>" + commit URL); any blockers.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- cowork-plugin-management:cowork-plugin-customizer: Customize a Claude Code plugin for a specific organization's tools and workflows. Use when: customize plugin, set up plugin, configure plugin, tailor plugin, adjust plugin settings, customize plugin connectors, customize plugin skill, tweak plugin, modify plugin configuration.
- cowork-plugin-management:create-cowork-plugin: Guide users through creating a new plugin from scratch in a cowork session. Use when users want to create a plugin, build a plugin, make a new plugin, develop a plugin, scaffold a plugin, start a plugin from scratch, or design a plugin. This skill requires Cowork mode with access to the outputs directory for delivering the final .plugin file.
- anthropic-skills:consolidate-memory: Reflective pass over your memory files — merge duplicates, fix stale facts, prune the index.
- anthropic-skills:docx: Use this skill whenever the user wants to create, read, edit, or manipulate Word documents (.docx files). Triggers include: any mention of 'Word doc', 'word document', '.docx', or requests to produce professional documents with formatting like tables of contents, headings, page numbers, or letterheads. Also use when extracting or reorganizing content from .docx files, inserting or replacing images in documents, performing find-and-replace in Word files, working with tracked changes or comments, or converting content into a polished Word document. If the user asks for a 'report', 'memo', 'letter', 'template', or similar deliverable as a Word or .docx file, use this skill. Do NOT use for PDFs, spreadsheets, Google Docs, or general coding tasks unrelated to document generation.
- anthropic-skills:mcp-builder: Guide for creating high-quality MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers that enable LLMs to interact with external services through well-designed tools. Use when building MCP servers to integrate external APIs or services, whether in Python (FastMCP) or Node/TypeScript (MCP SDK).
- anthropic-skills:pdf: Use this skill whenever the user wants to do anything with PDF files. This includes reading or extracting text/tables from PDFs, combining or merging multiple PDFs into one, splitting PDFs apart, rotating pages, adding watermarks, creating new PDFs, filling PDF forms, encrypting/decrypting PDFs, extracting images, and OCR on scanned PDFs to make them searchable. If the user mentions a .pdf file or asks to produce one, use this skill.
- anthropic-skills:pptx: Use this skill any time a .pptx file is involved in any way — as input, output, or both. This includes: creating slide decks, pitch decks, or presentations; reading, parsing, or extracting text from any .pptx file (even if the extracted content will be used elsewhere, like in an email or summary); editing, modifying, or updating existing presentations; combining or splitting slide files; working with templates, layouts, speaker notes, or comments. Trigger whenever the user mentions "deck," "slides," "presentation," or references a .pptx filename, regardless of what they plan to do with the content afterward. If a .pptx file needs to be opened, created, or touched, use this skill.
- anthropic-skills:schedule: Create or update a scheduled task that runs automatically. Use when the user says things like "every day", "each morning", "remind me in an hour", "run this at noon", or wants to reschedule an existing task.
- anthropic-skills:setup-cowork: Guided Cowork setup — install role-matched plugins, connect your tools, try a skill.
- anthropic-skills:skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- anthropic-skills:xlsx: Use this skill any time a spreadsheet file is the primary input or output. This means any task where the user wants to: open, read, edit, or fix an existing .xlsx, .xlsm, .csv, or .tsv file (e.g., adding columns, computing formulas, formatting, charting, cleaning messy data); create a new spreadsheet from scratch or from other data sources; or convert between tabular file formats. Trigger especially when the user references a spreadsheet file by name or path — even casually (like "the xlsx in my downloads") — and wants something done to it or produced from it. Also trigger for cleaning or restructuring messy tabular data files (malformed rows, misplaced headers, junk data) into proper spreadsheets. The deliverable must be a spreadsheet file. Do NOT trigger when the primary deliverable is a Word document, HTML report, standalone Python script, database pipeline, or Google Sheets API integration, even if tabular data is involved.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll start by reading the canonical doc and setting up the repo.

content.content:
1	# Using 1Password in this repo
2	
3	Two ways to pull secrets from 1Password instead of hardcoding them:
4	
5	- **1Password MCP server** — lets AI coding agents (Claude, Codex, etc.) read and manage 1Password items through tools.
6	- **`op` CLI** — lets humans, scripts, and CI read secrets and inject them into environment variables.
7	
8	Both authenticate with a **1Password Service Account token** and use **secret references** of the form `op://<vault>/<item>/<field>`.
9	
10	> **Never commit secrets.** Store them in 1Password and reference them with `op://...`. Keep real `.env` files out of git.
11	
12	## 1. 1Password MCP server (for AI clients)
13	
14	`@u2giants/1password-mcp` is an MCP server that exposes 1Password tools to MCP-compatible clients (Claude Desktop, Claude Code, VS Code, OpenAI Codex, etc.).
15	
16	Add it to your client config (example for Claude Desktop / Claude Code / VS Code):
17	
18	```json
19	{
20	  "mcpServers": {
21	    "1password": {
22	      "command": "npx",
23	      "args": ["-y", "@u2giants/1password-mcp"],
24	      "env": {
25	        "OP_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_TOKEN": "<your-service-account-token>"
26	      }
27	    }
28	  }
29	}
30	```
31	
32	OpenAI Codex (TOML), referencing an env var instead of storing the token in config:
33	
34	```toml
35	[mcp_servers."1password"]
36	command = "npx"
37	args = ["-y", "@u2giants/1password-mcp"]
38	env_vars = ["OP_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_TOKEN"]
39	```
40	
41	Once connected, the agent has tools including:
42	
43	- `vault_list`, `item_list`, `item_lookup`, `item_get`
44	- `password_read` (read a secret by `op://` reference or vault/item id)
45	- `password_create`, `password_update`, `password_generate`, `password_generate_memorable`
46	- `item_edit`, `item_delete`, `item_archive`, `note_create`
47	
48	Source & full docs: https://github.com/u2giants/1Password-MCP (npm: `@u2giants/1password-mcp`).
49	
50	## 2. `op` CLI (for humans, scripts, and CI)
51	
52	Install the 1Password CLI (`op`): https://developer.1password.com/docs/cli/get-started/
53	
54	Authenticate. For automation, use a Service Account token:
55	
56	```bash
57	export OP_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_TOKEN="<your-service-account-token>"   # macOS/Linux
58	```
59	```powershell
60	$env:OP_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_TOKEN = "<your-service-account-token>"   # Windows PowerShell
61	```
62	
63	(Interactive desktop users can instead run `op signin`.)
64	
65	Common commands:
66	
67	```bash
68	# Read a single secret to stdout
69	op read "op://<vault>/<item>/<field>"
70	
71	# List vaults / items
72	op vault list
73	op item list --vault "<vault>"
74	op item get "<item>" --vault "<vault>"
75	
76	# Run a command with secrets injected as env vars (values never touch disk)
77	op run -- <your-command>
78	
79	# Fill a template: .env.tpl contains FOO=op://vault/item/field
80	op inject -i .env.tpl -o .env
81	```
82	
83	Use `op://` references in committed templates (e.g. `.env.tpl`) and resolve them at runtime with `op run` / `op inject`, so real secret values never land in git.
84	
85	### Secret reference format
86	
87	```
88	op://<vault>/<item>/<field>
89	op://<vault>/<item>/<section>/<field>
90	```
91

content.content:
1	# Authentik IdP — designflow.app
2	
3	Central Identity Provider for all designflow.app applications. Runs at **https://auth.designflow.app**.
4	
5	Runs on **Alibaba Cloud (47.110.86.216)**. Migrated from Tencent Cloud 2026-05-27.
6	
7	This repo contains deployment configuration for the official Authentik upstream image, plus one custom service: the **access-matrix** Flask app for managing per-user per-app access.
8	
9	## What's live
10	
11	| Integration | Status |
12	|-------------|--------|
13	| AD sync via LDAP relay | Live — ~29 users from Edge-Authentik group in `iml.isaacmorris.com` synced on schedule |
14	| Brevo SMTP (password resets, notifications) | Live |
15	| Google SSO | Live — akadmin linked to `u2giants@gmail.com` |
16	| Microsoft 365 SSO | Live — akadmin linked to `albert@popcre.com`; AD users link by email on first M365 login |
17	| Coolify SSO (`coolify.designflow.app`) | Live — "Login with Authentik" button on Coolify login page |
18	| Twenty CRM SSO (`crm.designflow.app`) | Live — AD users sign in via SSO button |
19	| HiClaw auth (`control.claw.designflow.app`) | Live — oauth2-proxy protects the control UI |
20	| PopDAM SSO (`dam.designflow.app`) | Live — AD users auto-provisioned, no invitation required |
21	| Access Matrix (`matrix.designflow.app`) | Live — custom Flask UI for managing app access grants |
22	| HAProxy relay — edgesynology1 (primary) | Live |
23	| HAProxy relay — edgesynology2 (warm standby) | Live |
24	| Backrest backup (every 15 min, DO Spaces) | Live — 3 plans, DO Spaces repo, UI at :9898 |
25	
26	## Documentation
27	
28	| File | Contents |
29	|------|----------|
30	| `docs/architecture.md` | System design, component roles, LDAP topology, identity sources |
31	| `docs/configuration.md` | All env vars, LDAP/OIDC/OAuth config, HAProxy settings, API token |
32	| `docs/deployment.md` | Restart, upgrade, rollback, backup, HAProxy relay deployment |
33	| `docs/development.md` | How to make config changes, logs, SMTP testing |
34	| `docs/migration-tencent-to-alibaba.md` | Full migration playbook: Tencent Cloud → Alibaba Cloud (2026-05-27) |
35	| `AGENTS.md` | **Primary operating guide** — 14-section system inventory, quirks, incidents, pending work |
36	
37	## Repo layout
38	
39	```
40	docker-compose.yml       — Authentik service definition (source of truth for topology)
41	access-matrix/           — Custom Flask app for per-user per-app access management
42	  app.py                 — Flask app: /api/matrix and /api/toggle endpoints
43	  templates/index.html   — Single-page UI
44	  Dockerfile             — python:3.12-slim, gunicorn on port 5000
45	haproxy/
46	  haproxy.cfg            — HAProxy LDAP relay config (deployed to each relay machine)
47	  docker-compose.yml     — HAProxy deployment for Synology NAS
48	systemd/                 — host proxy units deployed to the VPS
49	  vpn-proxy.service      — microsocks SOCKS5 (:1080) in claudevpn ns + veth bridge
50	  vpn-http-proxy.service — tinyproxy HTTP proxy (:8888) — used by Authentik for SSO egress
51	  tinyproxy-vpn.conf     — tinyproxy config (→ /etc/tinyproxy/)
52	backup.sh                — Legacy pg_dump script (superseded by Backrest)
53	docs/                    — Extended documentation
54	.github/workflows/
55	  access-matrix.yml      — lint → test → build → GHCR → Coolify deploy on push to access-matrix/**
56	```
57	
58	## Quick reference
59	
60	- **Admin UI**: https://auth.designflow.app (username: `akadmin`, pk: 2657)
61	- **Coolify service UUID**: `zm80q1kbos0k8q0tlhyzsrs0`
62	- **GitHub repo**: https://github.com/u2giants/authentik
63	- **Compose changes require manual sync to Coolify** — see `docs/deployment.md`
64

content.text:
This changes the picture significantly. This is NOT a fork of upstream authentik — it's a small custom deployment-config repo owned by u2giants, and it HAS a documentation map: the "## Documentation" table in README.md (lines 26-36). So the least-surprising placement is `docs/1password.md` (the repo already uses `docs/`), and I should add it to the existing Documentation table rather than just a blockquote pointer.

The instructions said to add a blockquote pointer because they expected a big upstream fork with no "our" map. Since there IS our map, the right move is to register it there. I'll add a row to the Documentation table (the canonical pointer location for this repo) AND keep it minimal. Let me write the doc and update the README.

input.command: cd /c/repos/temp/1p/authentik && cp /c/repos/temp/1p/CANONICAL-1password.md docs/1password.md && echo "copied" && head -1 docs/1password.md

content.text: The doc is a verbatim copy. Now I'll register it in the existing Documentation table in README.md — that's the repo's real doc map, so a table row is the least-surprising pointer.

input.old_string: | `docs/migration-tencent-to-alibaba.md` | Full migration playbook: Tencent Cloud → Alibaba Cloud (2026-05-27) |
input.new_string:
| `docs/migration-tencent-to-alibaba.md` | Full migration playbook: Tencent Cloud → Alibaba Cloud (2026-05-27) |
| `docs/1password.md` | 1Password access for this repo — MCP server + `op` CLI |

content.text:
Done. PR opened successfully.

## Report

- **Repo:** u2giants/authentik (default branch `main`)
- **Fork?** **No** — `isFork:false`, no parent. This is NOT the large upstream authentik project; it's a small custom deployment-config repo (Authentik IdP for designflow.app) owned by u2giants. The least-intrusive fork assumptions in the brief didn't apply.
- **Placement:** `docs/1password.md` (verbatim copy of the canonical body, H1 `# Using 1Password in this repo` preserved). The repo already uses a `docs/` directory, so this was the least-surprising location.
- **Pointer location:** Instead of a top-of-README blockquote, I registered the doc as a row in the repo's existing **Documentation table** in `README.md` (under the `## Documentation` heading) — that table is this repo's actual doc map, so the table row is the canonical, least-surprising pointer here.
- **Map status:** **MAP FOUND** (not "NO MAP"). The README's `## Documentation` table is our team's maintained doc index; the new doc is linked there.
- **Delivery:** PR opened — **https://github.com/u2giants/authentik/pull/1** (branch `docs/1password-access` → `main`). Commit `9e64d91`, 2 files changed (doc + one README table row). No ruleset blockers; branch push and PR both succeeded normally.
- **Blockers:** None.

One deviation worth flagging: the brief was written expecting a big upstream fork with no team-owned doc map, prescribing a standalone file + a blockquote pointer. Since the repo is actually a small owned repo *with* a doc map, I added a Documentation-table row rather than a blockquote. If you'd prefer the literal blockquote instead, say so and I'll adjust the PR.
